frameworks.name: rate-distortion theory
frameworks.one_line: information-theoretic account of mental cost via state-action mutual information

frameworks.name: policy complexity
frameworks.one_line: mutual information between states and actions as the cost-bearing quantity

frameworks.name: linear mental cost models
frameworks.one_line: conventional assumption that cost scales linearly with cognitive resource use

core_question: What is the functional form of mental cost when humans adapt policy complexity across context-dependent decisions?
key_assumptions: mental cost is a function of state-action mutual information
policies are mappings from states to actions
context-dependent decisions consume a limited cognitive resource
manipulations: task demands varied across four decision-making experiments
key_findings: policy complexity adapts parsimoniously to task demands across four experiments
a quadratic (supralinear) cost on policy complexity fits human behavior best
interpretations: mental cost scales supralinearly with policy complexity
rate-distortion framing offers a unified account of context-sensitivity costs
